title: Ranunculus expansus
taxonomy: Ranunculus expansus is a species of flowering plant in the family Ranunculaceae. It is classified as a member of the genus Ranunculus and is a perennial herb. The plant belongs to the order Ranunculales and is native to the mountainous regions of the Himalayas and surrounding areas. Within its classification, Ranunculus expansus is most closely related to other species of the Ranunculus genus, such as Ranunculus glacialis and Ranunculus lyallii. The classification of Ranunculus expansus is based on its morphological characteristics and genetic relationships with other plants in the same genus.

side_effects: Ranunculus expansus has been found to be toxic and potentially harmful if ingested, as it contains toxic compounds called ranunculin and protoanemonin. These compounds can cause skin irritation, blistering, and burns upon contact with the plant's sap. Ingestion of the plant can lead to severe gastrointestinal symptoms, including vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain. If ingested in large quantities, Ranunculus expansus can be fatal due to its toxicity. It is essential to handle the plant with care and avoid any contact with its sap or roots.
intro: Ranunculus expansus, commonly known as the large-flowered buttercup, is a perennial plant species in the Ranunculaceae family. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antifungal properties, which can aid in wound healing and reduce inflammation. The bioactive constituents of Ranunculus expansus include ranunculin, protoanemonin, and alkaloids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant is made into herbal preparations such as tinctures, infusions, and salves, which are used to treat various conditions including skin irritations and infections.
